topics_covered: overview of Eagar's course catalog (Fusion Welding, Solid State Welding, Material Selection, Codes and Standards, Deformation Processing, Structural Life Assessment, Casting / Intro to Materials Processing)
iridium-clad plutonium RTGs in deep-space spacecraft (Voyager)
Eagar's pedagogical philosophy: no quizzes, no final exams, presentation or term paper or homework set
critique of quiz-driven education (high school math book anecdote)
the fluorescent light lecture (two-temperature plasma; electron vs. ion temperature; LED efficiency)
the Scotch tape lecture (viscous flow; Scotch tape vs. duct tape)
air conditioner as heat engine (Eagar's recurring example of MIT science education failing to teach context)
recurring_eagar_themes: MIT students are quiz-takers; this course breaks that mold
Moses-from-the-mountain anti-pedagogy (contrasted with Eagar's analogy-driven approach)
people_mentioned: Koichi Masubuchi (predecessor in MIT welding metallurgy)
Dr. Belmar (co-instructor, structural life assessment, material properties)
Simone (uses of structural materials, QC and joint capacity)
Christine, Jonathan, Jordan (students in the room, addressed by name)
cases_or_examples: fluorescent light vs. welding arc vs. LED (two-temperature plasma)
Scotch tape vs. duct tape (viscous flow adhesives)
notes: This is the opening lecture of the Spring 2014 Welding Metallurgy module. About half is administrative (course structure, modules, assignments, no-quizzes philosophy) and half is Eagar's pedagogical manifesto with two preview examples (fluorescent lights, Scotch tape). No deep technical content yet. Two-page-math-book anecdote and air-conditioner-as-heat-engine are characteristic Eagar set-pieces that recur across the corpus.
